FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study with 21 US sites, baseline ages 9-10 years, 11,868 participants recruited, analyzed through year 4 follow-up (2016-2022)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using latent class linear mixed models to identify trajectories of addictive screen use (SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ) and their associations with suicidal behaviors, ideation, and mental health outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA, 2025; original research reporting novel findings on addictive use trajectories

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Identified phone/SM-specific variables (6-item SMAQ, 8-item MPIQ, 6-item VGAQ measuring addictive use) paired with individual-trait outcomes. Multiple estimates: increasing social media addictive use trajectory RR=2.14 [95% CI 1.61-2.85] for suicidal behaviors; high-peaking social media RR=2.39 [95% CI 1.66-3.43] for suicidal behaviors; high video game trajectory associated with internalizing symptoms T score difference=2.03 [95% CI 1.45-2.61]

FT_IC3.evidence: Risk ratios with 95% confidence intervals reported for suicidal behaviors and ideation across all trajectory groups; mean differences in T scores for internalizing and externalizing symptoms

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results including risk ratios, 95% CIs, T score differences with statistical significance testing using Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design with SM as both IV (trajectories are predictors) and outcomes as DV (suicidal behaviors, mental health); trajectory-based IV analyses present throughout
notes: Uses validated addictive use questionnaires (SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ) as per Andreassen et al. and Walsh et al.; latent class linear mixed models identified 3 trajectories for social media and mobile phones, 2 for video games; models adjusted for baseline demographics and clinical characteristics; sensitivity analyses examined total screen time as covariate

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study longitudinal dataset with participants aged 9-10 at baseline; data from 21 US sites; analyzed at two timepoints (year 2 and year 4)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed models examining associations between VGA/SMA and neural responses during Monetary Incentive Delay (MID) fMRI task

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Computers in Human Behavior, 2025; original research examining bidirectional relationships between addictive behaviors and reward circuit function

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Identified phone/SM-specific variables (6-item VGAQ assessing video game addiction, 6-item SMAQ assessing social media addiction) paired with individual-trait neural outcomes. Numeric estimates reported: VGA × Time interaction in left nucleus accumbens b=0.106 [95% CI 0.040-0.172] p=0.002 for loss contrast; right amygdala and right ACC showed time × neural loss sensitivity interactions predicting later VGA (b=0.086 and b=0.088, respectively)

FT_IC3.evidence: Beta coefficients with 95% confidence intervals reported for VGA × Time and neural sensitivity × Time interactions; FDR-corrected p-values provided

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative neural response measures (beta-weights for reward and loss contrasts) across multiple brain regions (nucleus accumbens, amygdala, insula, ACC, OFC, vmPFC); LMM coefficients with statistical testing

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design examining both VGA and SMA with neural responses as outcomes; not SM-DV-only structure; bidirectional relationships tested
notes: VGAQ and SMAQ administered at year 2 and year 4; fMRI data from MID task used to assess neural responses to rewards and losses; final sample n=543 with complete data at both timepoints; LMM analysis included site as random effect and adjusted for head motion

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Release 5.1; longitudinal cohort data from 9,054 youth at 2-year follow-up (ages 11-12) and 4,007 youth at 4-year follow-up (ages 13-14); analyzed cross-sectional networks and cross-lagged panel networks

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using network estimation (graphical model with EBIC algorithm) and cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) modeling to examine centrality of problematic screen use, screen time, and phone-checking behavior within broader adolescent ecosystem

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2025; original study employing novel network methodology

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 2: Composite content check. Paper measures "problematic screen use, conceptualized as a continuous measure of screen-related functional impairment" across social media, gaming, and smartphones. Methods state: "Screen use obtained from self-report questionnaires, capturing both activity time and functional impairments." The problematic use composite is distinguished from total screen time. Step 1 yielded: Multiple numeric network centrality estimates including out-strength, bridge strength, and cross-lagged coefficients for problematic use predicting psychopathologies and environmental conditions. Example: Problematic use exhibited high out-strength with stronger connections to behavioral tendencies and psychopathology than screen time.

FT_IC3.evidence: Network centrality metrics (strength, out-strength, bridge strength) calculated for all nodes; cross-lagged coefficients estimated for autoregressive and cross-lagged paths

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative network measures reported including node centrality indices, edge strength differences, bridge strength estimates, and cross-lagged coefficients with statistical testing and bootstrapped confidence intervals

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-lagged panel network design with bidirectional pathways; screen use examined both as predictor and outcome across timepoints; not SM-DV-only longitudinal structure
notes: Network analysis using Gaussian graphical model (Extended Bayesian Information Criterion); problematic use measured as functional impairment severity (distinct from screen time); cross-sectional networks at T2 (n=9,054) and T4 (n=4,007); cross-lagged panel network with subset (n=3,954) for temporal dynamics; results show problematic use centrality exceeds screen time and checking behavior
